groundings.quote: deontology is one of those kinds of normative theories regarding which choices are morally required, forbidden, or permitted
within the domain of moral theories that assess our choices, deontologists—those who subscribe to deontological theories of morality—stand in opposition to consequentialists
within the domain of moral theories that assess our choices, deontologists—those who subscribe to deontological theories of morality—stand in opposition to consequentialists
Utilitarians, for example, identify the Good with pleasure, happiness, desire satisfaction, or “welfare” in some other sense
Deontologists of this stripe are committed to something like the doctrine of double effect, a long-established doctrine of Catholic theology (Woodward 2001)
deontological morality, in contrast to consequentialism, leaves space for agents to give special concern to their families, friends, and projects

Such norms are to be simply obeyed by each moral agent; such norm-keepings are not to be maximized by each agent. In this sense, for such deontologists, the Right is said to have priority over the Good
If any philosopher is regarded as central to deontological moral theories, it is surely Immanuel Kant
For Kant, the only thing unqualifiedly good is a good will (Kant 1785)
For Kant, the only thing unqualifiedly good is a good will (Kant 1785)
consequentialists generally agree that the Good is “agent-neutral” (Parfit 1984; Nagel 1986)

proposed_edges.quote: If any philosopher is regarded as central to deontological moral theories, it is surely Immanuel Kant
deontological moralities, unlike most views of consequentialism, leave space for the supererogatory
Somewhat orthogonal to the distinction between agent-centered versus patient-centered deontological theories are contractualist deontological theories
